task: Write a Python function trimmed_mean(nums) returning the mean of the list after dropping exactly one occurrence of the minimum and one occurrence of the maximum, as a float. An empty list returns None. Note one edge case it handles.
reason: The code correctly calculates the trimmed mean, but could be improved for readability.
trace: In this solution, I first handle the edge case where the input list is empty or contains fewer than 3 elements by returning None. Then, I sort the list and check if both minimum and maximum occurrences appear only once. If they do, I remove them from the list using `pop()`. After that, I filter out any `None` values from the list using `filter(None, nums)`. Lastly, I calculate the trimmed mean as the sum of remaining non-zero elements divided by the number of remaining elements minus 2 (since we removed one minimum and one maximum).
